content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: diagnose_401_spikes_post_refresh
C: grace_period_declared_but_unused ∧ refresh_overwrites_access_atomically ∧ validate_outside_lock ∧ module_lock_single_process_only ∧ no_old_token_retention ∧ concurrent_refresh_second_caller_gets_none ∧ clock_local_not_shared
P: root_cause("in_flight_requests_carry_old_access_token_after_store_save_replaces_pair") ∧ mechanism("validate_compares_pair_access_to_old_token_mismatch_returns_false") ∧ duration_match("30s_window_equals_unused_grace_period_constant") ∧ secondary("validate_reads_pair_without_lock_torn_read_vs_refresh_writer") ∧ secondary("module_lock_useless_across_gunicorn_workers_or_pods") ∧ secondary("double_refresh_race_loses_old_refresh_second_client_401s") ∧ fix("retain_prev_access_and_prev_access_exp_accept_within_grace") ∧ fix("move_serialization_to_store_cas_or_distributed_lock")
V: repro("two_parallel_requests_one_triggers_refresh_other_uses_old_access_observe_401") ∧ unit_test("validate_with_prev_access_within_grace_returns_true") ∧ unit_test("refresh_called_twice_with_same_refresh_is_idempotent") ∧ load_test("multi_worker_refresh_no_lost_update") ∧ metric("p401_rate_post_refresh_returns_to_baseline")
A: extend_tokenpair("add_prev_access_and_prev_access_exp_fields") ∧ patch_validate("accept_pair_access_or_prev_access_when_now_le_prev_access_exp") ∧ patch_refresh("set_prev_access_to_old_access_and_prev_access_exp_to_now_plus_grace") ∧ replace_module_lock("use_store_cas_or_redis_lock_keyed_by_user_id") ∧ make_refresh_idempotent("cache_new_pair_by_old_refresh_within_grace_window") ∧ add_metric("auth_refresh_race_401_total")

[AUDIT]
Root cause: GRACE_PERIOD=30 is declared but never consulted. When refresh() calls store.save(user_id, new), the old access string is immediately overwritten; any in-flight request still carrying the prior access token fails validate() because pair.access != access. The 30s spike window after renewal exactly matches the unused GRACE_PERIOD constant — dead code that was meant to implement a rolling acceptance window. Secondary issues: validate() reads the store with no lock while refresh() writes under a lock (torn read, stale TokenPair visible mid-rotation); _token_lock is a module-level threading.Lock that provides zero mutual exclusion across multiple Gunicorn workers, processes, or pods, so concurrent refreshes across workers race at the store; a double-submitted refresh token makes the second caller observe a rotated refresh value and get None → 401. clock=time.time is per-instance, so clock skew across pods can push access_exp comparisons inconsistent. Fix: add prev_access + prev_access_exp to TokenPair and accept either current or prev within grace in validate(); move serialization to the store (CAS or Redis lock per user_id); make refresh idempotent by returning the already-issued new pair when the same refresh token is replayed within the grace window.

The 30s spike window exactly matches the unused GRACE_PERIOD constant. Fix: add prev_access + prev_access_exp to TokenPair, accept either current or prev within grace in validate(); move serialization to the store (CAS or distributed lock per user_id); make refresh idempotent by returning the already-issued new pair when the same refresh token is replayed within the grace window.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: minimal_repro_of_refresh_race_401
C: single_user_id ∧ two_threads ∧ in_memory_store ∧ fixed_clock_or_real_clock ∧ no_network_required
P: arrange("issue_pair_for_user_keep_access_a_and_refresh_r") ∧ barrier("threading_event_to_release_both_at_once") ∧ thread_a("call_refresh_user_r_rotates_pair_stores_new") ∧ thread_b("call_validate_user_a_after_barrier_using_old_access") ∧ assert("thread_b_validate_returns_false_demonstrating_race") ∧ optional("loop_n_iterations_count_false_rate_to_show_window")
V: expect("assertion_fires_on_unpatched_code") ∧ expect("same_test_passes_after_grace_period_patch_applied") ∧ deterministic("barrier_guarantees_overlap_no_timing_flake")
A: write_test_file("tests/test_token_race.py") ∧ run("pytest_tests_test_token_race_py_dash_x")

[AUDIT]
Minimal deterministic repro — two threads released by a barrier so refresh rotates the pair while another request validates the old access token. On unpatched code, validate returns False and the assertion fires.

```python
# tests/test_token_race.py
import threading
import pytest
from auth.token_service import TokenService

class InMemoryStore:
    def __init__(self):
        self._d = {}
    def get(self, uid):
        return self._d.get(uid)
    def save(self, uid, pair):
        self._d[uid] = pair

def test_validate_fails_for_inflight_request_during_refresh():
    svc = TokenService(InMemoryStore())
    pair = svc.issue("u1")
    old_access, refresh = pair.access, pair.refresh

    start = threading.Event()
    result = {}

    def do_refresh():
        start.wait()
        svc.refresh("u1", refresh)

    def do_validate():
        start.wait()
        result["ok"] = svc.validate("u1", old_access)

    t1 = threading.Thread(target=do_refresh)
    t2 = threading.Thread(target=do_validate)
    t1.start(); t2.start()
    start.set()
    t1.join(); t2.join()

    # On unpatched code this assertion fires — old access is rejected
    # the instant refresh() commits the new pair, even though it has
    # not yet expired and we are well inside GRACE_PERIOD.
    assert result["ok"] is True, "in-flight request within grace must still validate"
```

To prove the 30s window specifically, wrap the validate call in a loop that advances a fake clock (`clock=lambda: now[0]`) and measure the fraction of False results for `t in [0, 30]` after refresh — it will be 100% on unpatched code, 0% after the prev_access + grace fix.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: minimal_fix_honor_grace_period_preserve_api
C: keep_tokenservice_method_signatures ∧ keep_tokenpair_public_fields ∧ add_optional_prev_fields_with_defaults ∧ no_new_dependencies ∧ single_process_scope_only
P: extend_tokenpair("add_prev_access_str_default_empty_and_prev_access_exp_float_default_zero") ∧ patch_validate("accept_current_access_until_access_exp_or_prev_access_until_prev_access_exp") ∧ patch_refresh("carry_old_access_as_prev_access_and_set_prev_access_exp_to_now_plus_grace_period") ∧ preserve_signatures("issue_validate_refresh_unchanged") ∧ verify_prior_test_now_passes("in_flight_validate_returns_true_within_grace")
V: pytest("tests/test_token_race.py") ∧ assert("validate_old_access_true_within_grace") ∧ assert("validate_old_access_false_after_grace_expiry") ∧ assert("validate_new_access_true") ∧ assert("refresh_return_type_still_tokenpair")
A: edit_file("auth/token_service.py") ∧ apply_snippet_below("tokenpair_plus_validate_plus_refresh")

[AUDIT]
Minimal fix: actually use GRACE_PERIOD. Carry the prior access string and a deadline on the TokenPair at refresh time; accept it in validate() until that deadline. Signatures of issue / validate / refresh are unchanged; new fields default to empty/zero so existing stores and callers still work.

```python
# auth/token_service.py
import time, secrets
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from threading import Lock

_token_lock = Lock()

@dataclass
class TokenPair:
    access: str
    refresh: str
    access_exp: float
    refresh_exp: float
    prev_access: str = ""
    prev_access_exp: float = 0.0

class TokenService:
    ACCESS_TTL = 900
    REFRESH_TTL = 86400
    GRACE_PERIOD = 30

    def __init__(self, store, clock=time.time):
        self.store = store
        self.clock = clock

    def issue(self, user_id):
        now = self.clock()
        pair = TokenPair(
            access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
            refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
        )
        self.store.save(user_id, pair)
        return pair

    def validate(self, user_id, access):
        pair = self.store.get(user_id)
        if pair is None:
            return False
        now = self.clock()
        if pair.access == access and now <= pair.access_exp:
            return True
        if pair.prev_access and pair.prev_access == access and now <= pair.prev_access_exp:
            return True
        return False

    def refresh(self, user_id, refresh):
        with _token_lock:
            pair = self.store.get(user_id)
            if pair is None or pair.refresh != refresh:
                return None
            now = self.clock()
            if now > pair.refresh_exp:
                return None
            new = TokenPair(
                access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
                refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
                prev_access=pair.access,
                prev_access_exp=now + self.GRACE_PERIOD,
            )
            self.store.save(user_id, new)
            return new
```

After this patch the previous-turn test passes: once refresh() commits the new pair, the old access string is still accepted by validate() for GRACE_PERIOD seconds because it is retained as prev_access with prev_access_exp = now + 30. Cross-worker serialization and refresh idempotency are out of scope for this minimal fix — they remain recommended follow-ups but are not needed to make the in-flight repro green.

```python
# auth/token_service.py
import time, secrets
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from threading import Lock

_token_lock = Lock()

@dataclass
class TokenPair:
    access: str
    refresh: str
    access_exp: float
    refresh_exp: float
    prev_access: str = ""
    prev_access_exp: float = 0.0

class TokenService:
    ACCESS_TTL = 900
    REFRESH_TTL = 86400
    GRACE_PERIOD = 30

    def __init__(self, store, clock=time.time):
        self.store = store
        self.clock = clock

    def issue(self, user_id):
        now = self.clock()

            access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),

            refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
        )

            return False
        now = self.clock()
        if pair.access == access and now <= pair.access_exp:
            return True
        if pair.prev_access and pair.prev_access == access and now <= pair.prev_access_exp:
            return True
        return False

    def refresh(self, user_id, refresh):
        with _token_lock:
            pair = self.store.get(user_id)
            if pair is None or pair.refresh != refresh:

            new = TokenPair(

                refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
```

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: monitor_and_canary_for_refresh_race_fix
C: fix_deployed_to_prod ∧ traffic_includes_refresh_flows ∧ auth_service_emits_metrics_and_structured_logs ∧ per_user_id_correlation_available
P: dashboard("auth_401_rate_segmented_by_time_since_last_refresh_bucket_0_to_30s_vs_baseline") ∧ dashboard("p99_401_per_user_within_30s_post_refresh_should_drop_to_near_zero") ∧ counter("auth_validate_prev_access_hit_total") ∧ counter("auth_validate_current_access_ok_total") ∧ counter("auth_validate_reject_mismatch_total") ∧ counter("auth_refresh_success_total_and_auth_refresh_reject_total") ∧ histogram("delta_seconds_between_last_refresh_and_401_event") ∧ log_sample("structured_401_with_fields_user_id_token_age_seconds_refresh_age_seconds")
V: pre_fix_baseline_captured("401_rate_post_refresh_t_minus_7d") ∧ post_fix_compare("401_rate_in_0_30s_window_returns_to_general_baseline") ∧ prev_access_hit_counter_nonzero_proves_grace_path_exercised ∧ no_regression_in_overall_refresh_success_rate
A: alert("canary_401_rate_in_30s_post_refresh_window_gt_baseline_plus_2_sigma_for_10m") ∧ alert("auth_validate_reject_mismatch_total_rate_step_change_gt_50_percent_vs_24h") ∧ alert("auth_validate_prev_access_hit_total_drops_to_zero_for_15m_while_refresh_volume_normal") ∧ alert("delta_seconds_p95_between_refresh_and_401_collapses_back_to_under_30s") ∧ synthetic_probe("every_1m_issue_refresh_validate_old_access_assert_200_within_grace")

[AUDIT]
Primary confirmation signal: slice the 401 rate by "seconds since this user's last refresh". Pre-fix, the 0–30s bucket was a sharp spike above baseline; post-fix it should flatten into the general baseline. A histogram of delta_seconds(last_refresh → 401_event) is the cleanest view — a concentrated mass under 30s is the fingerprint of this race, and it should disappear.

Emit three counters from validate(): auth_validate_current_access_ok_total, auth_validate_prev_access_hit_total, auth_validate_reject_mismatch_total. The middle one is new and proves the grace path is actually being exercised; if it is zero while refresh volume is normal, either the fix was reverted, the prev_access field is not being populated (store schema mismatch), or deploys are split across workers that still run the old code.

Canary alerts:
1. 401_rate_in_0_30s_post_refresh_window > baseline + 2σ sustained 10m → race is back.
2. auth_validate_reject_mismatch_total rate step-change > 50% vs 24h trailing → regression in validate path or store rollback.
3. auth_validate_prev_access_hit_total drops to 0 for 15m while auth_refresh_success_total is healthy → fix code path silently bypassed.
4. p95 delta_seconds(refresh → 401) collapses back under 30s → the spike pattern returned.
5. Synthetic probe every 1 minute: issue → refresh → validate(old_access); must return 200 within GRACE_PERIOD. One failure pages oncall; this is the deterministic tripwire independent of organic traffic.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: audit_auth_middleware_security
C: internal_service_behind_gateway ∧ gateway_strips("X-Forwarded-For") ∧ gateway_signs("X-Gateway-User-Id") ∧ hmac_over_uid_only_assumed ∧ no_strip_of("X-User-Id") ∧ no_strip_of("X-Gateway-User-Id")
P: finding1_auth_bypass_on_bad_sig ∧ finding2_legacy_header_unverified ∧ finding3_none_return_vs_unauthorized ∧ finding4_timing_attack_in_verify ∧ finding5_header_smuggling_from_client ∧ finding6_replay_no_nonce_or_ts ∧ finding7_silent_session_fallback
V: sig_fail_must_reject ∧ legacy_header_removed_or_signed ∧ resolve_user_raises("Unauthorized") ∧ hmac_compare_digest_used ∧ gateway_strips_client_identity_headers ∧ signature_binds("uid+nonce+ts") ∧ no_silent_anonymous_path
A: patch("return User on verify==False is critical bypass — must raise Unauthorized") ∧ patch("drop X-User-Id branch entirely or require HMAC") ∧ patch("replace return None with raise Unauthorized") ∧ patch("use hmac.compare_digest in _verify") ∧ patch("gateway must strip X-Gateway-User-Id, X-Gateway-Signature, X-User-Id from inbound") ∧ patch("sign uid||nonce||timestamp, reject stale/replayed")

[AUDIT]
Line `return User(id=gateway_uid)` after failed verify is a total auth bypass — any client can forge X-Gateway-User-Id with a junk signature and be trusted. Legacy X-User-Id path has no verification at all; if the gateway doesn't strip it (only X-Forwarded-For is documented as stripped), clients impersonate arbitrary users. resolve_user now returns None instead of raising Unauthorized — downstream code that treats None as "anonymous but allowed" becomes a silent privilege path. _verify is a black box: likely needs constant-time compare (hmac.compare_digest) to avoid timing oracle. HMAC over uid alone permits replay — bind a nonce+timestamp. Gateway must strip all identity headers inbound, not just XFF.

patch("drop X-User-Id branch entirely or require HMAC")
patch("replace return None with raise Unauthorized")
patch("gateway must strip X-Gateway-User-Id, X-Gateway-Signature, X-User-Id from inbound")
input.audit: Line `return User(id=gateway_uid)` after failed verify is a total auth bypass — any client can forge X-Gateway-User-Id with a junk signature and be trusted. Legacy X-User-Id path has no verification at all; if the gateway doesn't strip it (only X-Forwarded-For is documented as stripped), clients impersonate arbitrary users. resolve_user now returns None instead of raising Unauthorized — downstream code that treats None as "anonymous but allowed" becomes a silent privilege path. _verify is a black box: likely needs constant-time compare (hmac.compare_digest) to avoid timing oracle. HMAC over uid alone permits replay — bind a nonce+timestamp. Gateway must strip all identity headers inbound, not just XFF.

content:
X-Gateway-User-Id tells service who user is, but service itself never talked to user.

Gateway sits in front, authenticates session, then hands request inward with header saying "this is user 42."

Internal service trusts header because only gateway should set it. That trust only holds if two things true: gateway always strips same header from inbound client requests, and gateway signs header so downstream can verify.

Break either, whole model collapses.

If client can send X-Gateway-User-Id directly and gateway forwards it without stripping, client picks any user id. No login needed. Full impersonation.

HMAC signature exists to close that gap. Downstream checks signature, rejects anything not signed by shared key. Without verification, header value means nothing.

Problem in this diff: verification runs, but failure path still returns user anyway. Signature might as well not exist. Attacker sends header with garbage signature, gets in as whoever they want, including admin.

Second issue: legacy X-User-Id branch has no signature at all. Dead giveaway of old trust model where network boundary alone was deemed enough. Today assume attacker already inside, or finds SSRF, or hits service directly via misrouted ingress.

Third issue: gateway only documented as stripping X-Forwarded-For. Nobody confirmed it strips identity headers too. Must verify. Otherwise client smuggles header past gateway untouched.

Rule to carry forward: any header that grants identity must be cryptographically bound, stripped at trust boundary, and verified with fail-closed logic. If verification fails, request dies. Never degrades to "trust it anyway."

Identity headers without those three guarantees are just suggestions from attacker.

To: Leadership
From: Platform Security
Re: Auth middleware incident, post-mortem

What happened.

During recent migration toward gateway-signed identity headers, internal service auth middleware introduced logic flaw that allowed anyone reaching service to impersonate any user, including administrators.

Gateway in front of service was supposed to be sole authority on who user was. It attaches signed header identifying user. Service then checks signature before trusting header.

In new code, signature check ran, but regardless of result service still trusted header value. Equivalent to checking ID at door and letting person in whether or not ID matched photo.

In parallel, older header from prior trust model remained accepted with no verification at all. Any request containing that header gained identity for free.

Scope.

Vulnerability lived in internal auth path. Exploitation required network reach to service. No evidence of exploitation in production logs during window. Root cause identified within same day as discovery, patched, regression tests added.

What we learned.

Defense relies on two layers working together: gateway must strip client-supplied identity headers, and service must verify signatures and reject when verification fails. Silent fallback to trust broke second layer entirely. One layer alone never sufficient.

Code reviewers missed issue because fail-open branch looked similar to fail-closed branch. Two adjacent lines, one returning user on success, one returning user on failure. Visual similarity masked logical inversion.

Tests covered happy path only. No negative test asserting forged signature gets rejected. Absence of negative tests is absence of assurance.

Legacy code paths carry old assumptions. Migration that adds new mechanism without removing old one doubles attack surface.

Going forward.

Auth code changes now require mandatory security review from platform security before merge, not optional.

Every auth-related PR must include at least one test that proves rejection path works. Positive-only test suites will be blocked at review.

Inventory of identity headers across gateway and services, with documented contract for which are stripped, which are signed, which are verified. Gaps treated as incidents.

Deprecation schedule for legacy unsigned header within next quarter. No new code may read it.

Runbook update so on-call engineers know how to rotate HMAC keys and invalidate sessions under incident.

Training session for service owners on trust boundary design, using this incident as case study.

Ask.

Budget for one quarter of dedicated platform security engineering time to complete identity header inventory and deprecate legacy path. Without that, similar shape of bug likely recurs elsewhere in stack.
